cards.question: What are module bundlers doing?
cards.answer: Resolve dependency graph, transform (TS/JSX), bundle into optimized files, code split, tree shake, inject runtime, and output assets for deployment.
cards.questionLinked: What are [module bundlers](concept:module-bundlers) doing?
cards.answerLinked: Resolve dependency graph, transform (TS/JSX), bundle into optimized files, code split, tree shake, inject runtime, and output assets for deployment.

cards.question: How do you handle CSS in modern React apps?
cards.answer: CSS Modules, Tailwind, CSS-in-JS (styled-components, Emotion), or vanilla extract. Tailwind + CSS Modules is popular for performance and colocation. Tailwind v4 uses CSS-first config.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle CSS in modern React apps?
cards.answerLinked: [CSS Modules](concept:css-in-react), [Tailwind](concept:css-in-react), [CSS-in-JS](concept:css-in-react) ([styled-components](concept:css-in-react), Emotion), or vanilla extract. [Tailwind](concept:css-in-react) + [CSS Modules](concept:css-in-react) is popular for performance and colocation. [Tailwind](concept:css-in-react) v4 uses CSS-first config.

cards.question: What is PostCSS and why use it?
cards.answer: Tool for transforming CSS with plugins—Autoprefixer, nesting, Tailwind, cssnano minification. Part of most modern build pipelines.
cards.questionLinked: What is [PostCSS](concept:postcss) and why use it?
cards.answerLinked: Tool for transforming CSS with plugins—[Autoprefixer](concept:postcss), nesting, [Tailwind](concept:css-in-react), cssnano minification. Part of most modern build pipelines.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you manipulate the DOM efficiently?
cards.answer: Batch reads/writes (avoid layout thrashing), use `DocumentFragment`, delegate events, minimize reflows, prefer CSS classes over inline styles, and use virtual DOM frameworks for complex UIs.
cards.questionLinked: How do you manipulate the [DOM](concept:dom) efficiently?
cards.answerLinked: Batch reads/writes (avoid [layout thrashing](concept:reflow-repaint)), use `DocumentFragment`, delegate events, minimize [reflows](concept:reflow-repaint), prefer CSS classes over inline styles, and use [virtual DOM](concept:virtual-dom) frameworks for complex UIs.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What's the difference between `innerHTML` and `textContent`?
cards.answer: `innerHTML` parses and sets HTML—XSS risk with user input. `textContent` sets plain text only—safe for user content. Use `textContent` unless you need HTML rendering (then sanitize).
cards.questionLinked: What's the difference between [innerHTML](concept:inner-html) and [textContent](concept:text-content)?
cards.answerLinked: [innerHTML](concept:inner-html) parses and sets HTML—[XSS](concept:xss) risk with user input. [textContent](concept:text-content) sets plain text only—safe for user content. Use [textContent](concept:text-content) unless you need HTML rendering (then sanitize).

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How does event delegation work?
cards.answer: Attach one listener on a parent; use `event.target` to identify which child triggered the event. Efficient for dynamic lists—fewer listeners, works for future elements.
cards.questionLinked: How does [event delegation](concept:event-delegation) work?
cards.answerLinked: Attach one listener on a parent; use `event.target` to identify which child triggered the event. Efficient for dynamic lists—fewer listeners, works for future elements.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What's event bubbling vs capturing?
cards.answer: Capturing: event travels down from window to target. Target phase: at element. Bubbling: travels up from target to window. Default listeners run in bubbling phase; use `{ capture: true }` for capturing.
cards.questionLinked: What's [event bubbling](concept:event-bubbling-capturing) vs capturing?
cards.answerLinked: Capturing: event travels down from window to target. Target phase: at element. Bubbling: travels up from target to window. Default listeners run in bubbling phase; use `{ capture: true }` for capturing.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you prevent event propagation?
cards.answer: `event.stopPropagation()` stops bubbling/capturing to other elements. `stopImmediatePropagation()` also prevents other listeners on the same element.
cards.questionLinked: How do you prevent event [propagation](concept:event-bubbling-capturing)?
cards.answerLinked: `event.stopPropagation()` stops bubbling/capturing to other elements. `stopImmediatePropagation()` also prevents other listeners on the same element.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What's the difference between `preventDefault` and `stopPropagation`?
cards.answer: `preventDefault()` stops the browser's default action (form submit, link navigation). `stopPropagation()` stops the event from reaching other elements—it doesn't prevent default behavior.
cards.questionLinked: What's the difference between [preventDefault](concept:prevent-default) and [stopPropagation](concept:prevent-default)?
cards.answerLinked: `preventDefault()` stops the browser's default action (form submit, link navigation). `stopPropagation()` stops the event from reaching other elements—it doesn't [prevent default](concept:prevent-default) behavior.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you handle scroll events efficiently?
cards.answer: Throttle or debounce handlers, use `{ passive: true }` for touch/wheel listeners, prefer `IntersectionObserver` over scroll polling, and avoid layout reads in scroll handlers.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle scroll events efficiently?
cards.answerLinked: [Throttle](concept:debounce-throttle) or [debounce](concept:debounce-throttle) handlers, use `{ passive: true }` for touch/wheel listeners, prefer [IntersectionObserver](concept:intersection-observer) over scroll polling, and avoid layout reads in scroll handlers.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is the Intersection Observer API?
cards.answer: Observes when elements enter/exit viewport asynchronously—no main-thread scroll listeners. Used for lazy loading, infinite scroll, analytics visibility, and animations.
cards.questionLinked: What is the [Intersection Observer API](concept:intersection-observer)?
cards.answerLinked: Observes when elements enter/exit viewport asynchronously—no main-thread scroll listeners. Used for [lazy loading](concept:lazy-loading), infinite scroll, analytics visibility, and animations.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: When would you use `MutationObserver`?
cards.answer: When you need to react to DOM changes (added/removed nodes, attribute changes)—third-party widget integration, testing utilities, or legacy code without framework control.
cards.questionLinked: When would you use [MutationObserver](concept:mutation-observer)?
cards.answerLinked: When you need to react to [DOM](concept:dom) changes (added/removed nodes, attribute changes)—third-party widget integration, testing utilities, or legacy code without framework control.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How does `requestAnimationFrame` work?
cards.answer: Schedules a callback before the next repaint (~60fps). Ideal for animations and visual updates synced with the browser's render cycle—better than `setInterval` for DOM animation.
cards.questionLinked: How does [requestAnimationFrame](concept:request-animation-frame) work?
cards.answerLinked: Schedules a callback before the next [repaint](concept:reflow-repaint) (~60fps). Ideal for animations and visual updates synced with the browser's render cycle—better than [setInterval](concept:timers) for [DOM](concept:dom) animation.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What are Web Workers and when do you use them?
cards.answer: Run JavaScript on background threads—no DOM access. Use for CPU-heavy tasks: parsing large JSON, image processing, cryptography—keeping the main thread responsive.
cards.questionLinked: What are [Web Workers](concept:web-workers) and when do you use them?
cards.answerLinked: Run JavaScript on background threads—no [DOM](concept:dom) access. Use for CPU-heavy tasks: parsing large JSON, image processing, cryptography—keeping the main thread responsive.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is the difference between `localStorage` and `sessionStorage`?
cards.answer: Both store key-value strings (~5MB). `localStorage` persists until cleared. `sessionStorage` clears when the tab closes. Same-origin policy applies to both.
cards.questionLinked: What is the difference between [localStorage](concept:local-storage) and [sessionStorage](concept:session-storage)?
cards.answerLinked: Both store key-value strings (~5MB). [localStorage](concept:local-storage) persists until cleared. [sessionStorage](concept:session-storage) clears when the tab closes. Same-origin policy applies to both.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do cookies differ from web storage?
cards.answer: Cookies: sent with every HTTP request, ~4KB limit, can set expiry, `HttpOnly`/`Secure`/`SameSite` flags. Web storage: not sent automatically, larger limit, JS-only access (XSS risk).
cards.questionLinked: How do [cookies](concept:cookies) differ from web storage?
cards.answerLinked: [Cookies](concept:cookies): sent with [every](concept:array-some-every) HTTP request, ~4KB limit, can [set](concept:getters-setters) expiry, `HttpOnly`/`Secure`/`SameSite` flags. Web storage: not sent automatically, larger limit, JS-only access ([XSS](concept:xss) risk).

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What are the limitations of `localStorage`?
cards.answer: Synchronous (blocks main thread), string-only, ~5MB limit, same-origin, no automatic expiry, not accessible from Web Workers, XSS can read all data.
cards.questionLinked: What are the limitations of [localStorage](concept:local-storage)?
cards.answerLinked: Synchronous (blocks main thread), string-only, ~5MB limit, same-origin, no automatic expiry, not [accessible](concept:accessibility-a11y) from [Web Workers](concept:web-workers), [XSS](concept:xss) can read all data.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you handle browser compatibility?
cards.answer: `browserslist` config, Autoprefixer, progressive enhancement, feature detection (`if ('IntersectionObserver' in window)`), polyfills for critical features, and caniuse.com for support checks.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle browser compatibility?
cards.answerLinked: [browserslist](concept:browserslist) config, [Autoprefixer](concept:postcss), [progressive enhancement](concept:progressive-enhancement), feature detection (`if ('IntersectionObserver' in window)`), polyfills for critical features, and caniuse.com for support checks.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What are polyfills and when do you need them?
cards.answer: Code implementing features missing in older browsers. Need them when supporting legacy browsers for newer APIs (fetch, Promise, Array.flat). Modern apps often skip IE entirely.
cards.questionLinked: What are polyfills and when do you need them?
cards.answerLinked: Code implementing features missing in older browsers. Need them when supporting legacy browsers for newer APIs ([fetch](concept:fetch), [Promise](concept:promise), Array.flat). Modern apps often skip IE entirely.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How does the History API work?
cards.answer: `history.pushState()`/`replaceState()` change URL without reload. `popstate` event fires on back/forward. Foundation of SPA routing (React Router, Next.js client navigation).
cards.questionLinked: How does the [History API](concept:history-api) work?
cards.answerLinked: `history.pushState()`/`replaceState()` change URL without reload. `popstate` event fires on back/forward. Foundation of [SPA routing](concept:history-api) (React Router, Next.js client navigation).

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is CORS and how do you handle it?
cards.answer: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing—browser security blocking cross-origin requests unless server sends `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers. Fix on server; dev proxy for local development; never disable CORS in production client-side.
cards.questionLinked: What is [CORS](concept:cors) and how do you handle it?
cards.answerLinked: [Cross-Origin Resource Sharing](concept:cors)—browser security blocking cross-origin requests unless server sends `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers. Fix on server; dev proxy for local development; never disable [CORS](concept:cors) in production client-side.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What are CSP headers?
cards.answer: Content Security Policy restricts resource loading (scripts, styles, frames) to prevent XSS. Configure `script-src`, `style-src`, nonces/hashes for inline scripts. Critical for production security.
cards.questionLinked: What are [CSP](concept:content-security-policy) headers?
cards.answerLinked: [Content Security Policy](concept:content-security-policy) restricts resource loading (scripts, styles, frames) to prevent [XSS](concept:xss). Configure `script-src`, `style-src`, nonces/hashes for inline scripts. Critical for production security.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you implement drag and drop?
cards.answer: HTML5 Drag and Drop API (`draggable`, `dragstart`, `drop`) or pointer events for custom UX. Libraries: `@dnd-kit`, `react-beautiful-dnd`. Consider accessibility (keyboard alternatives).
cards.questionLinked: How do you implement drag and drop?
cards.answerLinked: HTML5 Drag and Drop API (`draggable`, `dragstart`, `drop`) or pointer events for custom UX. Libraries: `@dnd-kit`, `react-beautiful-dnd`. Consider [accessibility](concept:accessibility-a11y) (keyboard alternatives).

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What are Core Web Vitals?
cards.answer: Google's UX metrics: **LCP** (loading), **INP** (interactivity—replaced FID), **CLS** (visual stability). They affect SEO and measure real user experience.
cards.questionLinked: What are [Core Web Vitals](concept:core-web-vitals)?
cards.answerLinked: Google's UX metrics: **[LCP](concept:lcp)** (loading), **[INP](concept:inp)** (interactivity—replaced FID), **[CLS](concept:cls)** (visual stability). They affect SEO and measure real user experience.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you improve LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)?
cards.answer: Optimize hero images (preload, WebP, CDN), reduce TTFB (SSR/edge), eliminate render-blocking resources, use font-display: swap, and prioritize above-the-fold content.
cards.questionLinked: How do you improve [LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)](concept:lcp)?
cards.answerLinked: Optimize hero images (preload, WebP, CDN), [reduce](concept:array-map-filter-reduce) TTFB ([SSR](concept:server-side-rendering)/edge), eliminate render-blocking resources, use font-display: swap, and prioritize above-the-fold content.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What causes layout shifts (CLS)?
cards.answer: Images without dimensions, dynamically injected content, web fonts causing FOIT/FOUT, ads/embeds, and animations affecting layout. Fix with reserved space and `aspect-ratio`.
cards.questionLinked: What causes [layout shifts](concept:cls) ([CLS](concept:cls))?
cards.answerLinked: Images without dimensions, dynamically injected content, web fonts causing FOIT/FOUT, ads/embeds, and animations affecting layout. Fix with reserved space and `aspect-ratio`.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you optimize First Input Delay?
cards.answer: FID is replaced by **INP** in Core Web Vitals. Reduce long tasks, break up JS, use web workers, defer non-critical JS, and optimize event handlers for responsiveness.
cards.questionLinked: How do you optimize [First Input Delay](concept:inp)?
cards.answerLinked: FID is replaced by **[INP](concept:inp)** in [Core Web Vitals](concept:core-web-vitals). [Reduce](concept:array-map-filter-reduce) long tasks, break up JS, use [web workers](concept:web-workers), defer non-critical JS, and optimize [event handlers](concept:typescript-react-events) for responsiveness.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is the Critical Rendering Path?
cards.answer: Sequence: HTML → DOM, CSS → CSSOM, combine into render tree → layout → paint. Optimize by minimizing blocking resources and prioritizing above-the-fold content.
cards.questionLinked: What is the [Critical Rendering Path](concept:critical-rendering-path)?
cards.answerLinked: Sequence: HTML → [DOM](concept:dom), CSS → CSSOM, combine into render tree → layout → paint. Optimize by minimizing blocking resources and prioritizing above-the-fold content.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How does the browser render a page?
cards.answer: Parse HTML → DOM, parse CSS → CSSOM, render tree, layout (calculate geometry), paint (pixels), composite (layers). JS can trigger reflow/repaint at any stage.
cards.questionLinked: How does the browser render a page?
cards.answerLinked: Parse HTML → [DOM](concept:dom), parse CSS → CSSOM, render tree, layout (calculate geometry), paint (pixels), composite (layers). JS can trigger [reflow](concept:reflow-repaint)/repaint at any stage.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What blocks the rendering process?
cards.answer: Render-blocking CSS, synchronous JS in `<head>`, large DOM, expensive layout calculations, and fonts blocking text render.
cards.questionLinked: What blocks the rendering process?
cards.answerLinked: Render-blocking CSS, synchronous JS in `<head>`, large [DOM](concept:dom), expensive layout calculations, and fonts blocking text render.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is reflow vs repaint?
cards.answer: Reflow (layout): recalculates geometry—expensive. Repaint: redraws pixels without layout change—cheaper. Composite: GPU layer updates—cheapest. Minimize reflows.
cards.questionLinked: What is [reflow](concept:reflow-repaint) vs [repaint](concept:reflow-repaint)?
cards.answerLinked: [Reflow](concept:reflow-repaint) (layout): recalculates geometry—expensive. [Repaint](concept:reflow-repaint): redraws pixels without layout change—cheaper. Composite: GPU layer updates—cheapest. Minimize [reflows](concept:reflow-repaint).

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you minimize reflows?
cards.answer: Batch DOM reads/writes, use `transform`/`opacity` for animations (composite-only), avoid reading layout properties after writes, and use `DocumentFragment`.
cards.questionLinked: How do you minimize [reflows](concept:reflow-repaint)?
cards.answerLinked: Batch [DOM](concept:dom) reads/writes, use `transform`/`opacity` for animations (composite-only), avoid reading layout properties after writes, and use `DocumentFragment`.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What are the performance implications of CSS selectors?
cards.answer: Modern browsers optimize well—don't over-worry. Avoid overly deep universal selectors in huge DOMs. Prefer classes over complex descendant chains for maintainability.
cards.questionLinked: What are the performance implications of [CSS selectors](concept:css-selector-performance)?
cards.answerLinked: Modern browsers optimize well—don't over-worry. Avoid overly deep universal selectors in huge DOMs. Prefer classes over complex descendant chains for maintainability.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How does lazy loading work?
cards.answer: Defer loading off-screen images/resources until needed—native `loading="lazy"`, Intersection Observer, dynamic imports for JS, and route-based code splitting.
cards.questionLinked: How does [lazy loading](concept:lazy-loading) work?
cards.answerLinked: Defer loading off-screen images/resources until needed—native `loading="lazy"`, [Intersection Observer](concept:intersection-observer), [dynamic imports](concept:code-splitting) for JS, and route-based [code splitting](concept:code-splitting).

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is resource prioritization?
cards.answer: Browser assigns priority to resources (critical CSS high, lazy images low). Use `<link rel="preload">`, `fetchpriority="high"`, and early hints (103) to guide loading order.
cards.questionLinked: What is resource prioritization?
cards.answerLinked: Browser [assigns](concept:object-assign) priority to resources (critical CSS high, lazy images low). Use `<link rel="preload">`, `fetchpriority="high"`, and early hints (103) to guide loading order.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you implement progressive enhancement?
cards.answer: Build core functionality with HTML/CSS first, then enhance with JS. App works without JS (where possible), better accessibility, and graceful degradation on slow devices.
cards.questionLinked: How do you implement [progressive enhancement](concept:progressive-enhancement)?
cards.answerLinked: Build core functionality with HTML/CSS first, then enhance with JS. App works without JS (where possible), better [accessibility](concept:accessibility-a11y), and [graceful degradation](concept:progressive-enhancement) on slow devices.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is the RAIL performance model?
cards.answer: **R**esponse <100ms to input, **A**nimation 60fps (16ms/frame), **I**dle maximize idle time, **L**oad key content <1s. User-centric performance budgeting.
cards.questionLinked: What is the [RAIL performance model](concept:rail-performance-model)?
cards.answerLinked: **R**esponse <100ms to input, **A**nimation 60fps (16ms/frame), **I**dle maximize idle time, **L**oad key content <1s. User-centric performance budgeting.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you optimize for mobile devices?
cards.answer: Responsive design, touch targets ≥44px, reduce JS payload, optimize images, test on real devices, use network-aware loading, and monitor INP on mobile.
cards.questionLinked: How do you optimize for mobile devices?
cards.answerLinked: Responsive design, touch targets ≥44px, [reduce](concept:array-map-filter-reduce) JS payload, optimize images, test on real devices, use network-aware loading, and monitor [INP](concept:inp) on mobile.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What tools do you use for performance testing?
cards.answer: Lighthouse, WebPageTest, Chrome DevTools Performance, Calibre, Speedcurve, and production RUM (Vercel Analytics, Datadog RUM, Google CrUX).
cards.questionLinked: What tools do you use for performance testing?
cards.answerLinked: Lighthouse, WebPageTest, Chrome [DevTools Performance](concept:js-profiling), Calibre, Speedcurve, and production [RUM](concept:observability) (Vercel Analytics, Datadog [RUM](concept:observability), Google CrUX).

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What is Lighthouse and how do you use it?
cards.answer: Automated auditing tool for performance, accessibility, SEO, and best practices. Run in Chrome DevTools, CI (Lighthouse CI), or PageSpeed Insights. Use as guidance, not gospel.
cards.questionLinked: What is Lighthouse and how do you use it?
cards.answerLinked: Automated auditing tool for performance, [accessibility](concept:accessibility-a11y), SEO, and best practices. Run in [Chrome DevTools](concept:js-debugging-tools), CI (Lighthouse CI), or PageSpeed Insights. Use as guidance, not gospel.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you implement caching strategies?
cards.answer: HTTP cache headers (`Cache-Control`, `ETag`), CDN caching, service worker cache strategies (cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate), and TanStack Query for API caching.
cards.questionLinked: How do you implement caching strategies?
cards.answerLinked: HTTP cache headers (`Cache-Control`, `ETag`), CDN caching, [service worker](concept:service-worker) cache strategies (cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate), and [TanStack Query](concept:tanstack-query) for API caching.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: What are service workers?
cards.answer: Scripts running between network and app—enable offline support, push notifications, and advanced caching (PWAs). Register with `navigator.serviceWorker.register()`.
cards.questionLinked: What are [service workers](concept:service-worker)?
cards.answerLinked: Scripts running between network and app—enable offline support, push notifications, and advanced caching (PWAs). Register with `navigator.serviceWorker.register()`.

cards.part: Part 5: Web Fundamentals and Browser APIs
cards.question: How do you optimize network requests?
cards.answer: HTTP/2 multiplexing, bundle splitting, compression (brotli), CDN, prefetch/preconnect, GraphQL/tRPC to reduce over-fetching, debounce search, and cache with SWR patterns.
cards.questionLinked: How do you optimize network requests?
cards.answerLinked: HTTP/2 multiplexing, bundle splitting, compression (brotli), CDN, prefetch/preconnect, GraphQL/tRPC to [reduce](concept:array-map-filter-reduce) over-fetching, [debounce](concept:debounce-throttle) search, and cache with SWR patterns.

cards.question: How would you structure a large React application?
cards.answer: Feature-based folders (`features/auth`, `features/dashboard`), shared `components/ui`, `hooks`, `lib/utils`, `services/api`. Colocate tests. Separate server/client boundaries in Next.js App Router.
cards.questionLinked: How would you [structure a large React application](concept:feature-based-architecture)?
cards.answerLinked: Feature-based folders (`features/auth`, `features/dashboard`), shared `components/ui`, `hooks`, `lib/utils`, `services/api`. Colocate tests. Separate server/client boundaries in Next.js App Router.

cards.question: Where do you put business logic in a React app?
cards.answer: Custom hooks, service/API layers, domain modules—not in components. Components orchestrate UI; hooks/services encapsulate rules. Redux/Zustand for shared domain state.
cards.questionLinked: Where do you put business logic in a React app?
cards.answerLinked: [Custom hooks](concept:custom-hooks), service/API layers, domain modules—not in components. Components orchestrate UI; hooks/services encapsulate rules. Redux/Zustand for shared domain state.

cards.question: How do you handle cross-cutting concerns?
cards.answer: Middleware (logging, auth), React Context for theme/locale, HOCs/wrappers (legacy), higher-order hooks, error boundaries, and observability (Sentry, analytics) at app shell level.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle [cross-cutting concerns](concept:cross-cutting-concerns)?
cards.answerLinked: Middleware ([logging](concept:observability), auth), [React Context](concept:react-context-api) for theme/locale, HOCs/wrappers (legacy), higher-order hooks, [error boundaries](concept:error-boundary), and [observability](concept:observability) (Sentry, analytics) at app shell level.

cards.question: What's your approach to component composition?
cards.answer: Prefer composition over inheritance—compound components, render props, children slots. Small focused components assembled into features. Share behavior via hooks.
cards.questionLinked: What's your approach to [component composition](concept:react-composition)?
cards.answerLinked: Prefer [composition](concept:react-composition) over inheritance—compound components, render props, children slots. Small focused components assembled into features. Share behavior via hooks.

cards.question: When do you split a component into smaller pieces?
cards.answer: When it exceeds ~150–200 lines, has multiple responsibilities, re-renders are expensive, or sub-sections are reused elsewhere. Extract when readability suffers.
cards.questionLinked: When do you split a component into smaller pieces?
cards.answerLinked: When it exceeds ~150–200 lines, has multiple responsibilities, [re-renders](concept:react-re-render) are expensive, or sub-sections are reused elsewhere. Extract when [readability](concept:coding-standards) suffers.

cards.question: How do you decide between props and context?
cards.answer: Props for direct parent-child communication (1–2 levels). Context for widely shared, infrequently changing data. External store for frequently updated global state.
cards.questionLinked: How do you decide between props and [context](concept:use-context)?
cards.answerLinked: Props for direct parent-child communication (1–2 levels). [Context](concept:use-context) for widely shared, infrequently changing data. External store for frequently updated [global state](concept:react-context-api).

cards.question: What is the Container/Presenter pattern?
cards.answer: Container components handle data fetching and state; presenter components are pure UI receiving props. Now largely replaced by hooks—logic in custom hooks, UI in components.
cards.questionLinked: What is the [Container/Presenter pattern](concept:container-presenter-pattern)?
cards.answerLinked: Container components handle data fetching and state; presenter components are pure UI receiving props. Now largely replaced by hooks—logic in [custom hooks](concept:custom-hooks), UI in components.

cards.question: How do you implement dependency injection in React?
cards.answer: Context providers injecting services, prop injection, or module-level abstractions with test doubles. Example: `ApiProvider` with mock client in tests.
cards.questionLinked: How do you implement [dependency injection](concept:dependency-injection) in React?
cards.answerLinked: [Context](concept:use-context) providers injecting services, prop injection, or module-level abstractions with test doubles. Example: `ApiProvider` with mock client in tests.

cards.question: What are your thoughts on monorepos?
cards.answer: Great for shared packages (UI lib, utils, types) across apps. Tools: Turborepo, Nx, pnpm workspaces. Tradeoff: tooling complexity vs code reuse and atomic changes.
cards.questionLinked: What are your thoughts on [monorepos](concept:monorepo)?
cards.answerLinked: Great for shared packages (UI lib, utils, types) across apps. Tools: Turborepo, Nx, pnpm workspaces. Tradeoff: tooling complexity vs code reuse and atomic changes.

cards.question: How do you handle micro-frontends?
cards.answer: Split by domain (Module Federation, single-spa, iframe). Independent deploys, team autonomy. Challenges: shared dependencies, consistent UX, routing, and performance overhead.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle [micro-frontends](concept:micro-frontends)?
cards.answerLinked: Split by domain (Module Federation, single-spa, iframe). Independent deploys, team autonomy. Challenges: shared dependencies, consistent UX, routing, and performance overhead.

cards.question: What is island architecture?
cards.answer: Mostly static HTML with small interactive "islands" hydrated independently (Astro model). Minimal JS—good for content sites. Different from full SPA.
cards.questionLinked: What is [island architecture](concept:island-architecture)?
cards.answerLinked: Mostly static HTML with small interactive "islands" hydrated independently (Astro model). Minimal JS—good for content sites. Different from full SPA.

cards.question: How do you design a plugin system?
cards.answer: Define extension points (hooks/events), stable API contracts, sandboxing, version compatibility, and registration mechanism. React: render props, slot components, or dynamic imports.
cards.questionLinked: How do you design a plugin system?
cards.answerLinked: Define extension points (hooks/events), stable API contracts, sandboxing, version compatibility, and registration mechanism. React: render props, slot components, or [dynamic imports](concept:code-splitting).

cards.question: What's your approach to error handling architecture?
cards.answer: Error boundaries at route/feature level, global error handler for unhandled rejections, structured error types from API, user-friendly fallbacks, and Sentry/logging with context.
cards.questionLinked: What's your approach to error handling architecture?
cards.answerLinked: [Error boundaries](concept:error-boundary) at route/feature level, global error handler for [unhandled rejections](concept:unhandled-promise-rejection), structured error types from API, user-friendly fallbacks, and Sentry/logging with [context](concept:use-context).

cards.question: How do you structure API calls?
cards.answer: Dedicated API layer (fetch wrappers, TanStack Query hooks), typed responses, centralized error handling, retry logic, and separation from UI components.
cards.questionLinked: How do you structure API [calls](concept:call-apply-bind)?
cards.answerLinked: Dedicated [API layer](concept:api-layer) ([fetch wrappers](concept:api-layer), [TanStack Query](concept:tanstack-query) hooks), typed responses, centralized error handling, retry logic, and separation from UI components.

cards.question: Where does data transformation logic belong?
cards.answer: Between API layer and UI—selectors, mappers, or view-model hooks. Not in components. Keep components dumb; transform in hooks or `select` functions.
cards.questionLinked: Where does data transformation logic belong?
cards.answerLinked: Between [API layer](concept:api-layer) and UI—selectors, mappers, or view-model hooks. Not in components. Keep components dumb; transform in hooks or `select` functions.

cards.question: How do you ensure code maintainability?
cards.answer: Consistent conventions, TypeScript strict mode, code reviews, documentation for architecture decisions (ADRs), automated linting/formatting, and keeping components/hooks small and focused.
cards.questionLinked: How do you ensure code maintainability?
cards.answerLinked: Consistent conventions, TypeScript [strict mode](concept:strict-mode), code reviews, [documentation](concept:adr) for architecture decisions (ADRs), automated linting/formatting, and keeping components/hooks small and focused.

cards.question: What coding standards do you follow?
cards.answer: ESLint + Prettier, Conventional Commits, feature branches, PR templates, absolute imports, no `any` in TypeScript, and team-agreed naming conventions.
cards.questionLinked: What [coding standards](concept:coding-standards) do you follow?
cards.answerLinked: ESLint + Prettier, Conventional Commits, feature branches, PR templates, absolute imports, no `any` in TypeScript, and team-agreed naming conventions.

cards.question: How do you handle technical debt?
cards.answer: Track in backlog, allocate ~10–20% sprint capacity, prioritize debt causing bugs or blocking features, refactor incrementally (boy scout rule), and prevent new debt via reviews.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle [technical debt](concept:technical-debt)?
cards.answerLinked: Track in backlog, allocate ~10–20% sprint capacity, prioritize debt causing bugs or blocking features, refactor incrementally (boy scout rule), and prevent [new](concept:new-operator) debt via reviews.

cards.question: What's your approach to refactoring?
cards.answer: Small incremental changes with tests, strangler fig pattern for large rewrites, feature flags for safe rollout, and never big-bang rewrites without business justification.
cards.questionLinked: What's your approach to refactoring?
cards.answerLinked: Small incremental changes with tests, [strangler fig pattern](concept:strangler-fig-pattern) for large rewrites, [feature flags](concept:feature-flags) for safe rollout, and never big-bang rewrites without business justification.

cards.question: How do you implement feature flags?
cards.answer: LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or custom flags in config/DB. Wrap features in flag checks, gradual rollout, A/B testing, and kill switches for risky releases.
cards.questionLinked: How do you implement [feature flags](concept:feature-flags)?
cards.answerLinked: LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or custom flags in config/DB. Wrap features in flag checks, gradual rollout, A/B testing, and kill switches for risky releases.

cards.question: What is your branching strategy?
cards.answer: Trunk-based development with short-lived feature branches, or GitFlow for release-heavy products. PRs required, CI must pass, squash or merge commits per team preference.
cards.questionLinked: What is your [branching strategy](concept:git-branching)?
cards.answerLinked: [Trunk-based development](concept:git-branching) with short-lived feature branches, or [GitFlow](concept:git-branching) for release-heavy products. PRs required, CI must pass, squash or merge commits per team preference.

cards.question: How do you handle database migrations?
cards.answer: (Frontend angle:) Coordinate with backend on API versioning. For full-stack: migration tools (Prisma, Flyway), backward-compatible migrations, and never breaking API without deprecation period.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle [database migrations](concept:database-migrations)?
cards.answerLinked: (Frontend angle:) Coordinate with backend on API versioning. For full-stack: migration tools (Prisma, Flyway), backward-compatible migrations, and never breaking API without deprecation period.

cards.question: What's your deployment process?
cards.answer: CI pipeline: lint → test → build → deploy preview → manual/auto promote to production. Vercel/Netlify for frontend, blue-green or canary for zero-downtime.
cards.questionLinked: What's your [deployment process](concept:ci-cd)?
cards.answerLinked: CI pipeline: lint → test → build → deploy preview → manual/auto promote to production. Vercel/Netlify for frontend, blue-green or canary for zero-downtime.

cards.question: How do you implement blue-green deployments?
cards.answer: Two identical environments—deploy to inactive (green), run smoke tests, switch traffic via load balancer, keep blue for instant rollback.
cards.questionLinked: How do you implement [blue-green deployments](concept:blue-green-deployment)?
cards.answerLinked: Two identical environments—deploy to inactive (green), run smoke tests, switch traffic via load balancer, keep blue for instant rollback.

cards.question: What monitoring do you implement?
cards.answer: Error tracking (Sentry), RUM (Web Vitals), API latency, uptime checks, custom dashboards (Datadog/Grafana), and alerting on SLO breaches.
cards.questionLinked: What [monitoring](concept:observability) do you implement?
cards.answerLinked: Error tracking (Sentry), [RUM](concept:observability) (Web Vitals), API latency, uptime checks, custom dashboards (Datadog/Grafana), and alerting on SLO breaches.

cards.question: How do you handle logging in production?
cards.answer: Structured JSON logs, correlation IDs, log levels (error/warn in prod), no PII/secrets, centralized aggregation (Datadog, CloudWatch), and client-side error reporting to Sentry.
cards.questionLinked: How do you handle [logging](concept:observability) in production?
cards.answerLinked: Structured JSON logs, correlation IDs, log levels (error/warn in prod), no PII/secrets, centralized aggregation (Datadog, CloudWatch), and client-side error reporting to Sentry.

cards.question: What's your approach to documentation?
cards.answer: README for setup, Storybook for components, ADRs for decisions, inline JSDoc for public APIs, and runbooks for operations. Keep docs close to code.
cards.questionLinked: What's your approach to [documentation](concept:adr)?
cards.answerLinked: README for setup, Storybook for components, ADRs for decisions, inline JSDoc for public APIs, and runbooks for operations. Keep docs close to code.

cards.question: How do you onboard new developers?
cards.answer: Structured onboarding doc, codebase walkthrough, pair programming first tasks, documented dev environment setup, and assign a buddy for questions.
cards.questionLinked: How do you onboard [new](concept:new-operator) developers?
cards.answerLinked: Structured onboarding doc, codebase walkthrough, pair programming first tasks, documented dev environment setup, and [assign](concept:object-assign) a buddy for questions.

cards.question: What makes code readable?
cards.answer: Clear naming, small functions, consistent patterns, minimal nesting, meaningful comments for *why* not *what*, and code that reads like prose top-to-bottom.
cards.questionLinked: What makes [code readable](concept:coding-standards)?
cards.answerLinked: [Clear naming](concept:coding-standards), small functions, consistent patterns, minimal nesting, meaningful comments for *why* not *what*, and code that reads like prose top-to-bottom.
